ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Fish soup) used as closest defensible match; no specific Batang fish soup entry exists in FoodEx2 catalogue.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Batang All Fish Soup weighs approximately 500 g, comprising a generous double portion of Spanish mackerel fish slices in a clear savoury broth.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Fish, Spanish mackerel, raw (FDC ID 175136) adapted with clear fish broth dilution to reflect Singaporean fish soup composition
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for raw Spanish mackerel (FDC ID 175136), weighted to reflect a typical Singaporean fish soup composition of approximately 40% fish solids and 60% clear broth by weight. The input per_100g values (energy 64 kcal, protein 9 g, fat 2.4 g, carbohydrate 1.6 g) are consistent with this USDA-derived weighted estimate and have been retained. Sodium (240 mg/100 g) reflects broth seasoning contribution from light soy sauce in addition to the fish. Singapore HPB Foodo database was consulted for contextual validation of the overall dish profile; no exact composite dish entry for Batang All Fish Soup was found in HPB Foodo as of 2024.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — GI not applicable for negligible-carbohydrate fish-based soup
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (American Diabetes Association peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Batang All Fish Soup contains only 1.6 g carbohydrate per 100 g (8.0 g per 500 g serving). Per established GI methodology documented in Atkinson et al. (2008), glycemic index testing requires a minimum available carbohydrate load of 25–50 g; foods with negligible carbohydrate content are classified as not testable and GI is considered not applicable. Glycemic load per serving is effectively 0.0 and clinically insignificant. This peer-reviewed journal source is cited to document the methodological basis for the not_applicable classification.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 240 mg/100 g (1200 mg per 500 g bowl) is elevated; partially offset by estimated potassium of ~280 mg/100 g from Spanish mackerel and spring onions.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and no added salt to lower sodium intake, and may consume this dish in moderation given the meaningful potassium offset from the fish and aromatics.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate is very low at 1.6 g/100 g and sugar is negligible at 0.4 g/100 g; high protein at 9 g/100 g from Spanish mackerel slows gastric emptying and supports glycaemic stability.
type2_diabetes.guidance: This soup is well-suited for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its very low carbohydrate and high protein content; patients should avoid adding rice or noodles in large quantities, which would significantly raise the glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Spanish mackerel is rich in omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA); saturated fat is low at 0.6 g/100 g and trans fat is absent, supporting a favourable lipid profile.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The omega-3 EPA/DHA content of Batang fish actively supports cardiovascular health by reducing triglycerides and inflammation; patients should request minimal soy sauce to manage sodium, which is the primary cardiovascular concern in this dish.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Omega-3 EPA/DHA from Spanish mackerel reduces joint inflammation; ginger provides gingerols with evidence-based anti-inflammatory properties.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This dish is beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis due to its omega-3 and anti-inflammatory ginger content; request low-sodium soy sauce to avoid worsening RA-related tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Batang All Fish Soup is a Singaporean Chinese-style clear fish soup featuring a generous double portion of Batang (Spanish mackerel, Scomberomorus commerson) fish slices served in a light, savoury broth. The dish is prepared by simmering fresh fish slices in a clear stock seasoned with light soy sauce, ginger, spring onions, and a finishing drizzle of sesame oil. This version is prepared without meatballs, making it a pure fish-forward soup. The broth is clean and delicate with a mild umami depth, while the fish slices are tender and flaky with a subtly sweet oceanic flavour. Nutritionally, the soup provides 64 kcal per 100 g, with high protein at 9 g/100 g, low fat at 2.4 g/100 g, and very low carbohydrate at 1.6 g/100 g, making it an excellent low-calorie, high-protein meal. Spanish mackerel is a rich source of omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), which support cardiovascular and joint health. Sodium is moderate at 240 mg/100 g, primarily from soy sauce and broth. The dish is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management, cardiovascular health, and rheumatoid arthritis dietary protocols. It is commonly served as a hawker centre or food court meal in Singapore, typically accompanied by steamed white rice or plain rice porridge.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a bowl of fish soup?
Is fish soup good for diabetes?
Is Spanish mackerel soup low carb?
What is batang fish in English?
Is fish soup suitable for hypertension?
What is the difference between batang fish soup and seafood soup?
How much protein in fish soup?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for raw Spanish mackerel (FDC ID 175136) and clear fish broth, weighted to reflect a typical Singaporean fish soup composition of approximately 40% fish solids and 60% broth. The input values are consistent with this USDA-derived estimate. No exact HPB Foodo composite dish entry for 'Batang All Fish Soup' was identified; the closest HPB contextual match supports the overall nutritional profile. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted (not exact) source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Spanish mackerel raw (FDC ID 175136) and clear fish broth, weighted approximately 40% fish solids and 60% broth by weight. Input per_100g values retained as consistent with this USDA-derived estimate. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.7 g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.8 g) estimated from USDA Spanish mackerel fatty acid profile (FDC ID 175136), proportionally scaled to the soup composition. 3. potassium_mg (280 mg/100 g) estimated from USDA Spanish mackerel (~450 mg/100 g raw) and broth dilution; consistent with input record estimate. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific Singaporean fish soup preparation are not available as a composite dish entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024; individual raw ingredient values exist but reliable composite dish values cannot be confirmed without laboratory analysis. 5. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (1.6 g/100 g); gi_category set to 'not_applicable' and glycemic_index set to null per Veritas critical rule for low/zero-carbohydrate foods. GI source cites Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal to document the methodological basis. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/absent for this fish-based preparation per USDA Spanish mackerel data (FDC ID 175136). 7. Halal status noted as likely true for standard hawker preparation; kosher status not confirmed.
